data.title: Only 11 mega-caps stand out in SA Quant Ratings as markets hover near highs
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indices opened the week near record highs, reinforcing underlying strength in equities. Yet much of the market’s momentum is concentrated in a select group of names, according to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system.

Among U.S. mega-cap stocks, just 11 currently carry a “Buy” or “Strong Buy” rating under the model.

The narrow leadership underscores a more selective market backdrop, where investors are prioritizing companies with durable earnings and strong fundamentals. Seeking Alpha’s Quant framework ranks stocks using a data-driven approach that incorporates valuation, growth, profitability, and momentum—helping identify companies with sustained outperformance potential. 

Below are the 11 mega-cap stocks, ranked by their Quant ratings: 

  1. Micron Technology (MU), Quant Rating of 4.99. 
  2. Intel Corporation (INTC), Quant Rating of 4.99.
  3.  Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM), Quant Rating of 4.95.
  4. Chevron Corporation (CVX), Quant Rating of 4.94. 
  5. Texas Instruments (TXN), Quant Rating of 4.93. 
  6. Bank of America (BAC), Quant Rating of 4.91.
  7. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Quant Rating of 4.90.
  8. PepsiCo (PEP), Quant Rating of 4.64. 
  9. Philip Morris International (PM), Quant Rating of 4.45. 
  10. RTX Corporation (RTX), Quant Rating of 4.42. 
  11. AbbVie (ABBV), Quant Rating of 4.25.

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: UL Solutions added as a new long idea at Hedgeye
data.description:
UL Solutions was added as a new long idea at Hedgeye.

"ULS has performed strongly since its pre-IPO recommendation two years ago and remains a high-quality, long-term compounder," Hedgeye analyst Jay Van Sciver wrote in a note on Sunday. "Like other durable franchises that have transitioned to for-profit models, it still has meaningful runway to improve capital allocation through M&A, revenue growth, and margin expansion."

"Although the strategy briefly shifted toward more cyclical names heading into 2026, that view has reversed," Hedgeye's Van Sciver added. "The current macro backdrop and ULS’s ongoing consolidation and self-improvement story support re-engaging with the stock. The company remains a top long-term idea, with continued growth opportunities across industrial, consumer, energy, and AI/technology end markets, underpinned by strong brand equity and customer relationships."

UL Solutions (ULS) is expected to report Q1 results next Tuesday.  Shares of UL Solutions fell 1.7% on Monday.

data.title: Microsoft falls as OpenAI partnership evolves, says it will no longer pay revenue share
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) shares fell fractionally in early trading on Monday as the tech giant and OpenAI (OPENAI) said their partnership has continued to evolve, and OpenAI's license will become non-exclusive. 

“Today, we are announcing an amended agreement to simplify our partnership and the way we work together, grounded in flexibility, certainty and a focus on delivering the benefits of AI broadly,” Microsoft wrote in a statement on its website. “The greater predictability in the amended agreement strengthens our joint ability to build and operate AI platforms at scale while providing both companies the flexibility to pursue new opportunities.”

As part of the altered agreement, Microsoft will remain OpenAI's primary cloud partner, and OpenAI products will ship on Azure first. However, there is now a tweak that says if Microsoft “cannot and chooses not to support the necessary capabilities,” OpenAI can go elsewhere. 

Julian Lin, Investing Group Leader for Best Of Breed Growth Stocks, said the deal is actually a “net positive” for Microsoft, despite the share price reaction.

“While it does indeed explicitly allow OpenAI to work with other cloud providers, that isn't necessarily anything new and was inevitable,” Lin said. “The most important takeaway is that MSFT has locked in its 20% revenue share from OpenAI regardless of whether or not the company reaches AGI through 2030. This development appears to indicate that OpenAI is far further along than expected, with MSFT expected to benefit from both the revenue share as well as the cloud commitments over the long term.”

In addition, OpenAI can bring all of its products to customers across any cloud provider, such as Amazon (AMZN) Web Services. Microsoft will also continue to have a license to use OpenAI's intellectual property for its models and products through 2032, but the license will now be non-exclusive. 

Amazon shares gave up earlier gains and fell 0.8% in late morning trading following the news.

As part of the amended deal, Microsoft will no longer pay a revenue share to OpenAI, though Microsoft will receive revenue share payments from OpenAI through 2030, regardless of its technological progress. 

Microsoft is still a major shareholder of OpenAI, the Windows maker added.

Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian said the fact that an estimated 45% of Microsoft's AI backlog comes from OpenAI is a “major risk” for the Satya Nadella-led firm. However, it's likely any risks are already priced into Microsoft shares. 

“The market didn’t fully believe OpenAI would be able to pay for the previously agreed capacity, and for this reason, I don’t see this piece of news as majorly negative,” Ostian said via email.

Microsoft and OpenAI last amended their landmark agreement late in 2025, with a tweak in October to support its changing corporate structure.

(This story has been updated with additional analyst commentary.)

data.title: Tax refunds (temporarily) cushion consumers from gas price shock - Pantheon Macro
data.description:
Strong individual tax refunds are helping U.S. consumers weather the surge in gasoline (UNG) prices, but the support is expected to fade quickly as the normal filing season ends, according to a report from Pantheon Macroeconomics published Monday.

March tax refunds totaled $96B, up $14B from March 2025, thanks to tax cuts in the Trump administration's One Big Beautiful Bill Act, Pantheon economists Samuel Tombs and Oliver Allen wrote. That windfall has more than offset the hit from higher gas (UNG) prices, which have risen sharply amid the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

“The resulting near-1% lift to incomes is dominating the gas price shock for now,” the note said.

Retail gasoline sales rose $9B year-over-year in March but are projected to climb about $15B in April. The economists expect tax refunds will continue providing support through April, with daily data through April 22 pointing to refunds of just over $100B for the month, up roughly $20B from the prior year.

However, because the normal tax filing deadline passed on April 15, refund flows will slow sharply in May.

Pantheon projects consumer spending was relatively strong in March, rising 0.3%, driven by healthy goods spending including a jump in auto sales. Solid gains in furniture and electronics suggest households used refunds to fund big-ticket purchases.

Pantheon expects consumer spending in the second quarter will be little changed from the first quarter as the refund support fades while gas prices remain elevated.

data.title: Greenland Energy surges after signing Halliburton deal for Jameson drilling campaign
data.description:
Greenland Energy (GLND) pulls back after surging as much as 27% in early trading Monday after signing an agreement with Halliburton (HAL) for integrated consulting services and logistical management related to well and drilling services for its onshore campaign in the Jameson Basin.

Greenland Energy (GLND) said its agreement with Halliburton (HAL) forms a key component of its integrated Arctic operations strategy, ensuring best‑in‑class rig performance, logistics, and subsurface technology for the first onshore exploration well in the Jameson Basin.

Following more than a year of logistical planning and site preparation, Greenland Energy (GLND) said it expects to drill its first two wells in 2026.

The Jameson Land Basin spans ~2M acres and includes multiple identified targets supported by seismic data.

data.title: Sensient Technologies added as a new long idea at Hedgeye
data.description:
Sensient Technologies (SXT) was added as a new long idea at Hedgeye with the potential for 50% upside.

"Sensient Technologies is positioned to be a major beneficiary of the shift away from artificial food dyes, driven by a U.S. initiative to phase out petroleum-based additives," Hedgeye analyst Daniel Biolsi wrote in a note on Sunday. "This policy change is expected to create a durable tailwind for natural colors and flavors, with little likelihood of reversal despite political controversy."

Hedgeye's Biolsi added that "as the largest global producer of food colors, with strong capabilities in natural ingredients, supply chains, and farmer networks, Sensient is well placed to capture this demand shift. Converting customers from artificial to natural colors is complex and R&D-intensive but also highly lucrative: replacing $100M of artificial colors could translate into a $1B revenue opportunity."

Sensient (SXT) on Friday reported Q1 results that beat estimates.  Shares of Sensient ticked higher by 0.6% on Monday.

data.title: Williams-Sonoma now has ten brands with the Dormify launch
data.description:
Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) is up to operating ten brands now after launching Dormify, which the retailer set up to meet the evolving needs of students and young consumers navigating dormitories and compact living environments. Dormify was noted to offer a curated, modern assortment of stylish and highly functional home furnishings. 

Dormify will have a Pick-Up Campus Program that leverages the Williams-Sonoma (WSM) retail network of more than 450 stores. The program allows students to place orders and conveniently pick them up at store locations near campus on a preferred date. Customers who select Pick-Up Near Campus at checkout will be paired with a dedicated Dormify concierge to coordinate delivery and help avoid common issues associated with campus mailrooms, apartment buildings, and shared-living locations.

On a broad scale, the launch of Dormify underscores Williams-Sonoma's (WSM) continued investment in portfolio expansion and the company’s strategy to reach new customer segments through differentiated, lifestyle-driven brands. In particular, the recent strategic portfolio expansion positions Williams-Sonoma (WSM) to further capture the underpenetrated dorm and small-space living market by introducing a fresh, accessible design perspective—while upholding the quality standards and customer service offerings that define the company’s brands.

Williams-Sonoma's (WSM) other nine brands are Pottery Barn, Pottery Barn Kids, PBteen, West Elm, Williams Sonoma, Williams Sonoma Home, Rejuvenation, Mark and Graham, and GreenRow.

data.title: WTW creates new roles to lead AI strategy, adoption
data.description:
WTW (WTW), also known as Willis Towers Watson, named Spike Lipkin as its chief AI officer and Gordon Wintrob as head of AI Acceleration, both effective immediately, the company said Monday.

WTW (WTW) stock climbed 1.8% in late morning trading.

The appointments come after WTW's acquisition of Newfront in January as the company scales Newfront's AI-native approach and capabilities across WTW. The new roles will aim to foster faster, more consistent AI adoption at scale across WTW globally.

Lipkin is co-founder and former CEO of Newfront. Wintrob is co-founder and former chief technology officer of Newfront.

WTW (WTW) acquired Newfront for $1.05B  in upfront payments, including $900M in cash and $150M in equity. It also includes up to $250M in contingent consideration and up to $150M in equity payable after the third year if Newfront exceeds revenue goals.

data.title: Conflict in Middle East weighs on company coffers, clouds outlook
data.description:
As first-quarter earnings gather pace, companies from luxury retailers to travel and industrial giants are flagging warnings on the financial impact of the ongoing U.S.-Israel war with Iran on their balance sheet.

Despite hopes for robust ​first-quarter earnings, several companies so far have either cut guidance or issued financial warnings, underscoring rising pressure on their operations. The caution comes amid higher oil prices, ongoing supply chain disruptions, softer consumer confidence, and slowing growth, all of which are clouding their forecasts.

Already weighed down by tariff headwinds, companies are now facing fresh worries stemming from the ongoing Middle East conflict.

“Many U.S. and European companies are still reporting solid numbers, but the tone on earnings calls is clearly shifting. The issue is not necessarily demand today; it is the difficulty of forecasting costs, supply chains, energy prices, consumer confidence and investment decisions over the next few quarters,” said PP Foresight analyst Paolo Pescatore.

U.S. industrial giant Honeywell (HON) on Thursday forecast second-quarter ‌revenue below Wall Street estimates, citing the Middle East conflict. Honeywell CEO Vimal Kapur told analysts the conflict caused a 0.5% reduction in revenue during the first quarter and that he expects ​roughly a 1% drop during the second quarter, largely in its process automation and technology segment.

“The loss of revenue we're having in Middle East is also high-margin revenue. So that just has associated margin pressure because of services software, which is impacted the most due to disruptions there,” Kapur said.

Aluminium maker Alcoa (AA) said in the second-quarter, it expects the conflict in Iran to contribute to an unfavorable impact of $15M on adjusted EBITDA in its alumina segment. Earlier this month, oilfield services firm NOV (NOV) also warned that operational disruptions resulting from the Middle East war will weigh on its first quarter.

Oilfield services contractor Halliburton’s (HAL) COO Jeffrey Slocum emphasized on operational disruption saying “activity has been most impacted in the region's offshore markets in Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia and the land markets in Iraq and Kuwait” and outlined Q2 conflict impact of $0.07 to $0.09 per share.

Activity has been most impacted in the region's offshore markets in Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia and the land markets in Iraq and Kuwait

Several companies, during earnings calls, stressed on the fact that disruption in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical corridor for global oil supplies, has led to decreased visibility. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) warned higher prices for gas and chemicals due to the conflict could impact profitability. However, the chipmaker added it is too early to quantify the impact.

Similarly, JinkoSolar (JKS) said the conflict will have an impact on its shipment to the Middle East, even though the impact won’t be long-term. Industrial giant 3M (MMM) warned of about $125 million worth of annual cost impact due to higher oil prices.

Elevator maker Otis Worldwide (OTIS) said it experienced modernization project delays in EMEA due to the conflict in the first quarter. The company, however, believes that these delays are recoverable through the remainder of the year.

Several executives raised concern regarding the escalation of the war and tried to evaluate and explain the financial hit to their businesses.

Dow (DOW) used its first-quarter earnings call to deliver a blunt message to analysts: the conflict involving the U.S. and Iran, and the resulting disruption around the Strait of Hormuz, is no short-term nuisance, and it could lift costs through 2026.

Shares of ServiceNow (NOW) closed over 17% down after reporting that its first-quarter results were dented by delays in the Middle East. The company’s subscription revenue grew 19% in the quarter, but included a 75 basis point headwind from delayed closings of several large on-premise deals in the Middle East due to the conflict.

TRAVEL STOCKS TAKE A HIT

A surge in the jet fuel prices have already led to airlines companies hiking costs for checked-in baggage. Now, with these companies releasing quarterly results, investors are getting a clearer view of the financial impacts.

U.S. carrier United Airlines (UAL) spooked the market after lowering its profit outlook, while Alaska Air (ALK) suspended full-year guidance due to the uncertainty around higher fuel costs. American Airlines (AAL) also cut its 2026 profit forecast.

GE Aerospace (GE), a key supplier to airlines, gave a full-year earnings guidance that remained below consensus despite management’s upbeat tone. The Ohio-based company’s CEO Larry Culp told Reuters the company would have raised its forecast if not for the ​current uncertainty.

Lufthansa (DLAKF) said it will remove 20,000 short-haul flights through October as a result of jet fuel prices doubling since the outbreak of the Iran war.

 

EUROPE’S LUXURY FIRMS IN DISTRESS

With the global luxury industry already grappling with slowing growth, deteriorating spending by shoppers, especially in the Gulf areas, caused by the conflict has now dented hopes for a recovery in luxury demand.

French luxury goods group LVMH Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton (LVMHF) posted a 6% decline in quarterly sales, with the conflict having a 1% negative impact on organic growth.

LVMH's finance chief Cecile Cabanis said mall traffic in the Middle ‌East ⁠accounts for about 6% of group turnover.

“When the conflict started, and in the month of March, there was a shortfall and a deterioration of demand between 30% and 70%, depending on the malls, depending on the businesses,” Cabanis added.

Kering (PPRUF) said first-quarter sales ​at its Italian flagship brand Gucci dropped more than ‌expected, while Birkin bag maker Hermès International’s (HESAY) (HESAF) sales in the Middle East, its fastest-growing region in 2025, were down 6%.

Other European stocks also showed concerns.

Britain's top food retailer Tesco (TSCDY) warned that profits could fall amid war uncertainty. Airline company EasyJet (EJTTF) (ESYJY) reportedly said the Iran war and higher fuel prices are weighing on customer bookings.

With more companies preparing to post quarterly earnings, investors and analysts will be closely watching how this conflict unfolds in the upcoming weeks—and how businesses plan to navigate and mitigate the resulting financial pressures.

“Investors have likely priced in some near-term disruption, particularly in oil, freight and travel. What is less priced in is duration. If this remains contained, markets will look through many of these warnings. But if disruption persists for several quarters, guidance cuts will start to matter far more, especially for companies with weak pricing power or high exposure to energy, logistics and discretionary spending,” added Pescatore.

data.title: AMD slips as Northland Capital downgrades after massive run
data.description:
AMD (AMD) shares slipped 3.5% on Monday as Northland Capital downgraded the semiconductor company after its shares rose nearly 60% in a month. 

“We expect AMD to beat and raise estimates when it reports next week, but the results will likely not be as robust as Intel's,” analyst Gus Richard wrote in a note to clients. “While AMD will benefit from price increases, we believe AMD needs to continue spending heavily on R&D to catch up with NVDA.”

Richard lowered his rating on AMD to Market Perform from Outperform and kept his $260 price target on the stock.

Delving deeper, Richard said AMD's adjusted gross margins are in the mid-50% range, whereas Intel's (INTC) are in the high-30% to low-40% range. And with Nvidia (NVDA) using the supply chain as a competitive weapon, buying capacity ahead of competitors, AMD could be in a precarious position, with good results, but “not as good” as Intel's.

“We believe the CY27 consensus needs to come down, as we expect AI infrastructure spending to decline in CY27,” Richard added. “We believe that CAPEX/cash flow of the hyperscalers is maxed out, and as OpenAI and Anthropic go public this year, they will demonstrate greater financial discipline. They are already moving away from unlimited free usage toward strict usage caps, token-volume-based pricing, and higher-tier subscriptions. We think this slows AI demand growth. At the same time, construction delays are affecting data center facilities.”
